q: What are the three broad categories of substances transported by the cardiovascular system?
a: 1. Nutrients, water, and gases (from external environment). 2. Materials moving from cell to cell. 3. Wastes eliminated by the cells.

q: Cardiovascular Transport Function: What is the source and destination for Oxygen?
a: Source: Lungs. Destination: All cells.

q: Cardiovascular Transport Function: What is the source and destination for Nutrients and Water?
a: Source: Intestinal tract. Destination: All cells.

q: Cardiovascular Transport Function: What is the source and destination for Wastes moving cell-to-cell?
a: Source: Some cells. Destination: Liver for processing.

q: Cardiovascular Transport Function: What is the source and destination for Immune cells, antibodies, and clotting proteins?
a: Source: Present in blood continuously. Destination: Available to any cell that needs them.

q: Cardiovascular Transport Function: What is the source and destination for Hormones?
a: Source: Endocrine cells. Destination: Target cells.

q: Cardiovascular Transport Function: What is the source and destination for Stored nutrients?
a: Source: Liver and adipose tissue. Destination: All cells.

q: Cardiovascular Transport Function: What is the source and destination for Metabolic wastes?
a: Source: All cells. Destination: Kidneys.

q: Cardiovascular Transport Function: What is the source and destination for Heat?
a: Source: All cells. Destination: Skin.

q: Cardiovascular Transport Function: What is the source and destination for Carbon dioxide?
a: Source: All cells. Destination: Lungs.

q: What are Capillaries?
a: Microscopic blood vessels where exchange of materials between blood and interstitial fluid occurs.

q: What are Arteries?
a: Blood vessels that carry blood away from the heart.

q: What are Veins?
a: Blood vessels that return blood to the heart.

q: Anatomical Location and Function of the Septum?
a: Location: Central wall of the heart. Function: Divides the heart into left and right halves to prevent mixing of oxygenated and deoxygenated blood.

q: Anatomical Location and Function of the Atrium?
a: Location: Upper chambers of the heart. Function: Receives blood returning to the heart from blood vessels.

q: Anatomical Location and Function of the Ventricle?
a: Location: Lower chambers of the heart. Function: Pumps blood out into the blood vessels.

q: What is Pulmonary circulation?
a: The blood vessels that go from the right ventricle to the lungs and back to the left atrium.

q: What is Systemic circulation?
a: The blood vessels that carry blood from the left side of the heart to the tissues and back to the right side of the heart.

q: What is the Aorta?
a: A large artery that receives oxygenated blood pumped out of the left ventricle.

q: What are the Superior and Inferior Vena Cava?
a: The large veins where blood pressure is the lowest; they empty deoxygenated blood into the right atrium.

q: Trace the pathway of deoxygenated blood (Pulmonary Circulation flow).
a: Vena cava -> Right atrium -> Right ventricle -> Pulmonary trunk / Pulmonary arteries -> Lungs.

q: Trace the pathway of oxygenated blood (Systemic Circulation flow).
a: Lungs -> Pulmonary veins -> Left atrium -> Left ventricle -> Aorta -> Body tissues.

q: Anatomical Location and Function of the Pericardium?
a: Location: Encasing the heart. Function: A tough membranous sac that protects the heart and contains lubricating fluid.

q: Anatomical Location and Function of the Myocardium?
a: Location: Forms the bulk of the heart wall. Function: Cardiac muscle that provides the contractile force of the heart.

q: Anatomical Location and Function of the Pulmonary trunk?
a: Location: Arises from the right ventricle. Function: The major artery that receives deoxygenated blood to send to the lungs.

q: Anatomical Location and Function of Atrioventricular (AV) valves?
a: Location: Between the atria and ventricles. Function: Prevent backward flow of blood from ventricles into atria during ventricular contraction.

q: Anatomical Location and Function of Semilunar valves?
a: Location: Between the ventricles and the major arteries (aorta/pulmonary trunk). Function: Prevent backward flow of blood from arteries into ventricles during ventricular relaxation.

q: Anatomical Location and Function of Papillary muscles?
a: Location: Moundlike extensions of ventricular muscle. Function: Provide stability for the chordae tendineae to prevent valve prolapse during contraction.

q: Anatomical Location and Function of the Tricuspid valve?
a: Location: Right side of the heart (between right atrium and right ventricle). Function: Right-sided AV valve with three flaps that prevents backflow into the right atrium.

q: Anatomical Location and Function of the Bicuspid (mitral) valve?
a: Location: Left side of the heart (between left atrium and left ventricle). Function: Left-sided AV valve with two flaps that prevents backflow into the left atrium.

q: Anatomical Location and Function of the Aortic valve?
a: Location: Between the left ventricle and the aorta. Function: Semilunar valve preventing backflow into the left ventricle.

q: Anatomical Location and Function of the Pulmonary valve?
a: Location: Between the right ventricle and the pulmonary trunk. Function: Semilunar valve preventing backflow into the right ventricle.

q: What is the Coronary circulation?
a: The special blood supply consisting of blood vessels feeding the cardiac muscle itself.

q: Anatomical Location and Function of the Right coronary artery (RCA)?
a: Location: Runs from the aorta around the right side of the heart. Function: Feeds the right atrium, most of the right ventricle, and the posterior interventricular septum.

q: Anatomical Location and Function of the Left coronary artery (LCA)?
a: Location: Left side of the heart. Function: Divides into the circumflex and anterior interventricular branches to supply the left atrium, left ventricle, and interventricular septum.

q: Anatomical Location and Function of the Coronary sinus?
a: Location: Major vein on the posterior aspect of the heart. Function: Empties venous blood directly into the right atrium.

q: What is the primary importance of the heart valves?
a: They ensure the one-way, unidirectional flow of blood through the heart.

q: What are Contractile cells and what is their function?
a: The typical cardiac muscle cells that contract in response to depolarization signals to provide the pumping force of the heart.

q: What are Autorhythmic cells (pacemakers) and what is their function?
a: Specialized myocardial cells that generate action potentials spontaneously and set the rate of the heartbeat.

q: How are myocardial cells arranged and connected in the heart?
a: Cells branch and join via intercalated disks, which contain desmosomes for transferring force and gap junctions for electrical connectivity.

q: What is the key role of pacemaker cells in initiating action potentials without innervation?
a: They are "myogenic," meaning the signal for contraction originates within the heart muscle itself. They spontaneously depolarize to generate action potentials that spread rapidly to contractile cells.

q: What are the first 5 steps of Excitation-Contraction (EC) Coupling in cardiac muscle?
a: 1. Action potential enters from adjacent cell. 2. Voltage-gated L-type Ca2+ channels open, Ca2+ enters. 3. Ca2+ induces Ca2+ release through RyR channels in SR. 4. Local release causes a Ca2+ spark. 5. Summed Ca2+ sparks create a Ca2+ signal.

q: What are the final 5 steps of Excitation-Contraction (EC) Coupling and Relaxation in cardiac muscle?
a: 6. Ca2+ binds to troponin to initiate contraction. 7. Relaxation occurs when Ca2+ unbinds from troponin. 8. Ca2+ is pumped back into SR via Ca2+-ATPase. 9. Ca2+ is exchanged with Na+ by the NCX antiporter. 10. Na+ gradient is maintained by Na+-K+-ATPase.

q: What is Calcium-induced calcium release (CICR)?
a: The process where the initial influx of extracellular Ca2+ through voltage-gated channels triggers the release of more Ca2+ from the sarcoplasmic reticulum.

q: What is the role of RyR channels in myocardial EC-coupling?
a: They are ryanodine receptor-channels in the sarcoplasmic reticulum that open in response to Ca2+ influx, releasing stored Ca2+ into the cytosol (CICR).

q: What is the role of the Na+-Ca2+ antiporter (NCX) in myocardial relaxation?
a: It exchanges 1 Ca2+ out of the cell for 3 Na+ into the cell to remove calcium from the cytosol during relaxation.

q: What is the role of the sodium-potassium pump (Na+-K+-ATPase) in myocardial relaxation?
a: It maintains the necessary Na+ concentration gradient by pumping out the Na+ that entered via the NCX antiporter.

q: What occurs during Phase 4 (Resting membrane potential) of a cardiac contractile cell action potential?
a: The resting membrane potential is stable at roughly -90 mV.

q: What occurs during Phase 0 (Depolarization) of a cardiac contractile cell action potential?
a: Depolarization occurs due to voltage-gated Na+ channels opening and a rapid influx of Na+.

q: What occurs during Phase 1 (Initial repolarization) of a cardiac contractile cell action potential?
a: Initial repolarization begins as Na+ channels close and fast K+ channels open, allowing K+ efflux.

q: What occurs during Phase 2 (Plateau) of a cardiac contractile cell action potential?
a: The plateau phase results from an increase in Ca2+ permeability (influx via L-type channels) and a decrease in fast K+ permeability.

q: What occurs during Phase 3 (Rapid repolarization) of a cardiac contractile cell action potential?
a: Rapid repolarization occurs as Ca2+ channels close and slow K+ channels open, allowing K+ efflux to return the cell to resting potential.

q: What is the refractory period in a cardiac contractile cell?
a: The long period (due to the plateau phase) during which the cell cannot be re-stimulated.

q: Why can't cardiac muscle undergo summation and tetanus?
a: The long refractory period lasts almost as long as the entire muscle twitch, preventing the sustained contraction (tetanus) and allowing the heart to relax and fill with blood.

q: What is a pacemaker potential?
a: The unstable membrane potential of autorhythmic cells that starts at -60 mV and slowly depolarizes toward threshold.

q: What is the role of If channels (funny current / HCN channels) in autorhythmic cells?
a: They are permeable to K+ and Na+. Net influx of Na+ slowly depolarizes the cell toward threshold during the pacemaker potential phase.

q: What causes the steep depolarization phase in an autorhythmic cell action potential?
a: At threshold, voltage-gated Ca2+ channels open, causing a steep depolarization due to Ca2+ influx.

q: What happens during repolarization of an autorhythmic cell action potential?
a: Slow K+ channels open, resulting in an efflux of K+ that repolarizes the cell.

q: How does the resting potential differ between contractile and autorhythmic cells?
a: Contractile cells have a stable resting potential around -90 mV. Autorhythmic cells have an unstable pacemaker potential starting at -60 mV.

q: How does the depolarization phase differ between contractile and autorhythmic cells?
a: Contractile cells depolarize via a rapid influx of Na+. Autorhythmic cells depolarize steeply via an influx of Ca2+.

q: Anatomical Location and Function of the Sinoatrial (SA) node?
a: Location: Right atrium. Function: The main pacemaker of the heart that sets the pace of the heartbeat.

q: What is the function of the Internodal pathways?
a: They connect the SA node to the AV node, conducting the electrical signal.

q: Anatomical Location and Function of the Atrioventricular (AV) node?
a: Location: Near the floor of the right atrium. Function: Delays the transmission of action potentials to allow atria to complete contraction before ventricular contraction begins.

q: What is the Atrioventricular bundle (AV bundle / bundle of His)?
a: A pathway that passes the electrical signal from the AV node down the ventricular septum.

q: What are the Bundle branches?
a: Pathways that split from the AV bundle and continue downward to the apex along the left and right sides of the ventricular septum.

q: What are Purkinje fibers?
a: Fibers that spread outward from the apex among the contractile cells to rapidly transmit impulses throughout the ventricular muscle wall.

q: What is the AV node delay and why is it important?
a: The AV node slows down the transmission of action potentials, which allows the atria to finish contracting and filling the ventricles before ventricular contraction begins.

q: Define Diastole and Systole.
a: Diastole is the time during which cardiac muscle relaxes. Systole is the time during which the muscle contracts.

q: Cardiac Cycle Phase 1: What happens during Late diastole (The heart at rest)?
a: Atrial and ventricular diastole occurs. The heart is at rest, allowing passive filling of the ventricles (accounts for 80% of filling).

q: Cardiac Cycle Phase 2: What happens during Atrial systole (Completion of ventricular filling)?
a: Atria contract, forcing a small amount of additional blood (active filling, about 20%) into the ventricles, reaching End-Diastolic Volume (EDV).

q: What is End-diastolic volume (EDV)?
a: The total volume of blood in the ventricle at the end of ventricular relaxation/filling (the maximum volume).

q: Cardiac Cycle Phase 3: What happens during Isovolumic ventricular contraction?
a: Ventricular muscle begins to contract against closed valves. AV valves close, blood volume doesn't change, and pressure increases.

q: What causes the first heart sound ("lub")?
a: The closing of the AV valves during early (isovolumic) ventricular contraction.

q: Cardiac Cycle Phase 4: What happens during Ventricular ejection?
a: Semilunar valves open, and blood is actively ejected into the arteries, leaving behind the End-Systolic Volume (ESV).

q: What is End-systolic volume (ESV)?
a: The volume of blood remaining in the ventricle at the end of ventricular contraction (the minimum volume).

q: Cardiac Cycle Phase 5: What happens during Isovolumic ventricular relaxation?
a: Ventricular muscles relax, pressure drops rapidly, and semilunar valves close. No blood enters or exits because ventricular pressure is still higher than atrial pressure.

q: What causes the second heart sound ("dup")?
a: The closing of the semilunar valves during isovolumic ventricular relaxation.

q: Define Waves, Segments, and Intervals on an ECG.
a: Waves: Parts of the trace that go above/below baseline. Segments: Sections of baseline between two waves. Intervals: Combinations of waves and segments.

q: What does the P wave represent on an ECG?
a: Depolarization of the atria.

q: What does the P-R segment (or P-Q segment) represent?
a: Conduction time through the AV node and the AV bundle. During this time, the mechanical event of atrial contraction occurs.

q: What does the QRS complex represent on an ECG?
a: The progressive wave of ventricular depolarization (and masked atrial repolarization).

q: What does the S-T segment represent?
a: The period between ventricular depolarization and repolarization. During this time, the mechanical event of ventricular contraction occurs.

q: What does the T wave represent on an ECG?
a: Repolarization of the ventricles.

q: What is the relationship between pressure changes and flow through the heart?
a: Blood flows down pressure gradients. The ventricles generate high driving pressure during contraction, forcing blood out into arteries. Pressure drops during relaxation, allowing blood to flow from atria into ventricles.

q: What is Stroke Volume (SV) and how is it calculated?
a: The volume of blood pumped by one ventricle during one contraction. SV = EDV - ESV.

q: What is Ejection Fraction and how is it calculated?
a: The percentage of EDV ejected with one contraction. Formula: Stroke Volume / EDV.

q: What is Cardiac Output (CO) and how is it calculated?
a: The volume of blood pumped by one ventricle in a given period of time (per minute). CO = heart rate x stroke volume.

q: What is Preload and the Frank-Starling law of the heart?
a: Preload is the degree of myocardial stretch before contraction. The Frank-Starling law states that stroke volume is proportional to EDV; the heart pumps all the blood that returns to it.

q: What is Venous return and what factors affect it?
a: The amount of blood that enters the heart from the venous circulation (determines EDV). It is affected by the skeletal muscle pump, respiratory pump, and sympathetic constriction of veins.

q: Define Contractility, Inotropic agents, and Afterload.
a: Contractility: Intrinsic ability of a muscle fiber to contract. Inotropic agents: Chemicals that affect contractility. Afterload: Combined load of EDV and arterial resistance during contraction.

q: How does the Parasympathetic nervous system control heart rate at the cellular level?
a: It decreases heart rate via Acetylcholine on muscarinic receptors, which increases permeability to K+ (efflux) and decreases permeability to Ca2+.

q: How does the Sympathetic nervous system control heart rate at the cellular level?
a: It increases heart rate via Norepinephrine/Epinephrine on Beta-1-adrenergic receptors, which increases permeability to Na+ and Ca2+ (opening If and Ca2+ channels via cAMP).

q: What is the role of Phospholamban in sympathetic control of contractility?
a: Catecholamines use phospholamban to enhance Ca2+-ATPase activity in the sarcoplasmic reticulum, concentrating Ca2+ to allow for stronger and shorter contractions.

q: END
a: END
